summary: Jeff Atwood visits the Internet Archive headquarters in San Francisco and makes an impassioned case for its importance to digital civilization. He describes the organization's evolution from archiving web pages to attempting to archive everything—books, music, films, and physical media. The post emphasizes how linkrot destroys internet history within just five years, making the Archive's Wayback Machine essential. Atwood calls on readers to donate money or contribute obscure tech materials like shareware CDs and computer magazines. He frames the Internet Archive as a modern Library of Alexandria, driven by common good rather than commercial interests.
key_points: The Internet Archive has expanded far beyond web snapshots to include physical books, live music, films, and scanned texts—attempting to archive all human knowledge
Linkrot is pervasive and devastating; even five-year-old blog posts have numerous dead links that would be lost forever without the Wayback Machine
The Archive operates as a non-profit motivated by common good, unlike commercial entities with ulterior motives
They actively seek donations of obscure materials—shareware CDs, computer magazines, zines, pamphlets—that wouldn't normally end up in libraries
With 30 employees in San Francisco and 200 worldwide, plus massive storage infrastructure, the scale of the mission requires community support
money_quotes: Without the Internet Archive, the Internet would have no memory.
The Internet Archive isn't merely archiving the Internet any more, they are attempting to archive everything.
Civilization is developing severe amnesia as a result; indeed it may have become too amnesiac already to notice the problem properly.
The Internet Archive is the beginning of a cure – the beginning of complete, detailed, accessible, searchable memory for society.
There's seriously nothing we don't want. I don't question. I take it in, I put it in items. I am voracious. Omnivorous. I don't say no.

It is an unprecedented kind of open source archiving, not driven by Google or Microsoft or some other commercial entity with ulterior motives, but a non-profit organization motivated by nothing more than the obvious common good.
key_insight: Digital civilization is developing collective amnesia as information disappears faster than it can be preserved, and the Internet Archive stands as our primary defense against losing our shared history.
